ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for raita or mixed raita; closest parent 'yogurt-based food product' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated raita term; 'Yoghurt, plain' (A0CKP) used as closest parent. Mix raita is a spiced, vegetable-enriched yogurt preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for raita or mixed raita as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of mix raita is approximately 120 g, presented as a small bowl alongside biryani, kebabs, or Indian flatbreads.

nutrient_source.title: Raita (plain yogurt with vegetables) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, composite estimate for yogurt-based raita
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain dahi (curd/yogurt) at approximately 60 kcal, 3.1 g protein, 4.0 g fat, 3.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Mix raita is yogurt diluted with chopped vegetables (cucumber, tomato, onion, carrot) and spices. A standard formulation of approximately 70% yogurt and 30% mixed vegetables yields the adapted values used here. Sodium is elevated slightly by added salt and black salt. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) and published Indian dietary composition data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dairy foods and yogurt-based preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mix raita. Plain yogurt (full-fat) has a published GI of approximately 36 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Mix raita is predominantly yogurt (~70%) with low-GI vegetables and spices, which do not materially raise the GI. GI of 36 is applied as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (36 × 4.2 g carbohydrate × 1.2) / 100 = approximately 1.8 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and black salt
hypertension.guidance: Mix raita contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g due to added table salt and black salt (kala namak); individuals with hypertension should request a low-salt preparation or limit portion size to one small bowl (120 g) to keep sodium contribution below 250 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mix raita has a low glycemic index (~36) and only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per standard serving, making it a suitable accompaniment for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole-milk yogurt; beneficial calcium and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mix raita provides modest saturated fat (~1.7 g per 100 g) from whole-milk yogurt; substituting low-fat yogurt reduces saturated fat further, and the dish's potassium (155 mg/100 g) and calcium content support vascular health, making it a heart-friendly condiment in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mix Raita is a traditional Indian yogurt-based condiment and side dish prepared by combining plain dahi (curd/yogurt) with a mixture of finely chopped or grated raw vegetables such as cucumber, tomato, onion, and carrot, seasoned with roasted cumin powder, black salt (kala namak), fresh mint, coriander leaves, and optionally green chilli. The dish is served chilled and has a cool, creamy, mildly tangy flavour with subtle spice notes from cumin and black salt. The texture is smooth and thick from the yogurt base, punctuated by the crunch of fresh vegetables. Mix raita is a staple accompaniment to biryani, pulao, kebabs, parathas, and other Indian main courses, serving both as a palate cleanser and a digestive aid. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-glycaemic food (~52 kcal per 100 g, GI ~36) that provides calcium (~105 mg/100 g), protein (~2.6 g/100 g), and probiotics from the fermented yogurt base. Its low glycaemic index and modest carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate due to added salt and should be monitored by those with hypertension. In Singapore, mix raita is commonly served at Indian vegetarian restaurants and North Indian cuisine establishments, including at Boat Quay dining venues.
common_user_queries: What vegetables are in mix raita?
How much sodium is in raita?
What is the GI of raita?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for mix raita exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dahi (curd) data combined with standard vegetable composition data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central plain whole-milk yogurt. The formulation (70% yogurt, 30% mixed vegetables) is representative of standard restaurant-style mix raita. GI is an analogous estimate based on published yogurt GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN plain dahi and USDA FoodData Central plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284), adjusted for a standard mix raita formulation of approximately 70% yogurt and 30% mixed vegetables (cucumber, tomato, onion, carrot). 2. Sodium is elevated relative to plain yogurt due to added table salt and black salt; restaurant preparations may vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for dahi/yogurt-based preparations. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in plain whole-milk yogurt per USDA FoodData Central. 7. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on published GI for plain full-fat yogurt (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 8. This dish is served at Indian restaurants in Singapore including Jewel Of India (Boat Quay) and Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry (Boat Quay) per field survey data. 9. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore Indian restaurant context.
